title: Behind the Scenes of the Empire

chapters.title: How Ukrainians closed Russia's window onto Europe
chapters.text:
To get a real sense of how Ukrainian and Russian cultures have been tangled together, you have to go back to 1654. Hetman Bohdan Khmelnytskyi was in a bind. He had won real ground against the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, and he had a degree of autonomy, but he could not just declare independence and crown himself a legitimate ruler. He did not come from the royal or the Kniaz families, so he faced a hard choice. One road led back into the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. Another led to the Ottoman Empire. The third led to the Muscovite Empire.

The Zaporozhian people had been fighting the Polish crown for years, and that fight tipped the scales. The Muscovite Empire offered support, protection, and the promise of sovereignty. The Pereyaslav Council is usually treated as the moment when the future of a free Ukraine was sealed. Time and again, Ukrainian writers have come back to that crossroads and cursed not only the wrong turn but Khmelnytskyi himself for making it.

The seventeenth century started off well. Trade, military, scientific, and educational cooperation began to take shape. Russians brought in Ukrainian intellectuals and cultural figures to build up their scientific and religious institutions. The Ukrainians who crossed the border were inspired by the ideas of the Enlightenment, and they went in with the naive hope that they would be able to plant and grow their own values.

The Muscovite Empire eventually became the Russian Empire, and Ukraine was treated as a conquered colony with rich resources.

Inside the Russian Empire, only a small slice of the population could read or write, and almost no one knew Latin. Because the Ukrainians brought in Western European ideas, they were treated as heretics. Priests arriving from Ukraine were forced to be rebaptized and had to burn their theological books. Russians destroyed the Uchytelne Yevanheliie, the Didactic Gospel, written by the Ukrainian scholar and writer Kyrylo Trankvilion-Stavrovetskyi. By the time Peter I came along, obsessed with Europeanizing his realm, he was desperate for Ukrainian help.

Ukrainians became educational missionaries. From the mid-1600s on, books, projects, ideas, and priceless knowledge traveled from Kyiv to Moscow and St. Petersburg. Thousands of Ukrainian scientists, cultural figures, and religious leaders went to the empire on educational missions, founding schools, churches, monasteries, and research institutions. Simeon Polotsky, a graduate of the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy, founded the Slavic-Greek-Latin Academy, the first institution of higher learning in Moscow.

The best and brightest students at Russian institutions of higher learning went to Kyiv, because no Russian university could match the academic level of the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy. The descendants of the Tatar-Mongol yoke, though, struggled not only to adopt new ideas but to even take in the finer points of Western thought.

The people of the Russian Empire looked at Ukrainian scientists and theologians with deep suspicion and treated them as heretics. There were cases of visiting priests being rebaptized in the "correct" Russian rite, and locals burned valuable theological works, since they could not read Latin.

chapters.title: Colonizers who lagged behind on culture
chapters.text:
Ukrainian cultural and scientific missionaries handed the gift of knowledge to the people of the Russian Empire, and the response was anything but grateful. The empire grew quickly and absorbed every gain it could on its own soil, without caring about the nationality of the authors, violently folding every inhabitant into the label "Russian people." It was the ideology of the cradle of fraternal Slavic peoples, and the supposed reunification of those peoples inside the Russian Empire and later the USSR, that the Soviet Union picked up and ran with.

Ukrainians woke up to the fact that the promised sovereignty and equality were a lie. The intellectual class turned to ethnography and folklore to draw a clear line between themselves and the colonizers.

Ethnography and folkloristics turned into political sciences. They sharpened the differences between peoples and pushed back against the imperial Russian ideology, especially the notion that Ukrainians were inferior.

Building a Ukrainian identity under constant oppression and forced denationalization was tough. The imperial invaders, though, almost helped the cause. By acting as the "other," they forced Ukrainians to recognize how different the two groups really were. The image of the "other" is a key ingredient in any national identity.

For Ukrainians, the "moskal" became the other, the distilled symbol of everything that was unpleasant, foreign, and unacceptable. The moskal became a kind of shorthand for Great Russians and their culture and behavior. Ukrainian artists built the image of the moskal into a deeply unattractive figure, someone who was almost biologically different from Ukrainians.

You can find moskals in the pages of Ukraine's greatest literature. One of the most famous is Kateryna by Taras Shevchenko. The main character is a young woman who meets a Russian officer and is won over by his sweet talk. Naivety and trust cost her dearly, since the officer left her with their child and went back to his military service. The disgraced woman became an outcast, rejected by her community and by her mother. Crushed by shame and grief, she drowned herself in a pond.

Kateryna is read as a parable for Ukrainian-Russian cultural relations. Just like the young woman, Ukraine was seduced by the oaths and eloquent promises of Muscovy, then used and betrayed in the same casual way. History shows that there is no point in screaming about broken promises to moskals, since they have always behaved as dishonorable "foreign folk" who do not treat anyone right.

The English translation of Kateryna by John Weir was used as the source here.

The Ukrainian writer Oksana Zabuzhko coined the phrase Kateryna's paradigms to describe the powerless appeal to abusers, the act of reminding them of the promises they never kept.

chapters.title: The habit of living two lives
chapters.text:
In that period, the life of an artist was far from easy. Every creator had to choose. They could serve the empire, become a tool of propaganda, and enjoy the easy life of the secular elite, or they could, at the cost of their safety and status, fight the planted ideology and stand up for Ukraine. Some took the imperial route, others stood and died for the second path, and the rest tried to play both sides.

One of the most passionate Ukrainian patriots was Taras Shevchenko, who hated Muscovites with every fiber of his being and cursed Khmelnytskyi for choosing the wrong side. Not all of his peers shared that radical stance. Hryhorii Kvitka-Osnovianenko, the founder of Ukrainian classic prose, and Ivan Kotliarevsky, the pioneer of modern Ukrainian literature, both painted Muscovites with irony and mockery, drawing a sharp line between the two peoples.

That did not stop them from writing odes to Russian rulers when it suited them. Unlike Shevchenko, who flatly refused to accept defeat and disappear into the empire, they treated that kind of double loyalty as just part of the new reality. They could only speak freely in private letters, where they tended to be sharper and more radical about the occupiers.

The trap of a dual identity, part Ukrainian and part imperial, haunted many talented people. Mykola Hohol, whose achievements were shamelessly claimed by Russia, is a textbook example of a split artist. He was a native of Poltava, a descendant of Ivan Skoropadsky and Petro Doroshenko, both Cossack political and military leaders, raised in the traditions of Ukrainian baroque culture. Hohol drew a firm line between "us" (Ukrainians) and "them" (Russians) in his writing, treating Ukrainian features with tenderness and Muscovites with open hostility.

Even so, given the uncertain future of his homeland, Hohol chose to write in Russian. In private letters, he confessed to feeling torn, unsure of where his soul really belonged.

Hohol came to see Ukrainian political sovereignty as a lost cause, so he poured energy into preserving Ukrainian cultural independence. Censors savaged the first pro-Ukrainian version of Taras Bulba, ripping out every reference to Ukraine and replacing it with "South Russian region." They also slipped in new paragraphs about the Orthodox faith and the arrival of the Russian Tsar.

The same pattern shows up in Hohol's unfinished Mazepa's Reflections, where he writes about Ukraine as an independent state that became a victim of Russian oppression.

As a Mazepa ally, Hohol was hated by Muscovites, so he never fit the myth of being Pushkin's pupil and the father of the natural school in Russian prose. The Mazepa's Reflections extract stayed hidden away.

A small piece of cultural context. In Ukrainian mythology, the word "dvodushnyk" refers to a being with two souls, one human and one demonic. Writers who were torn between Russian narratives and their own loyalties were labeled "dvodushnyky," duplicitous demons.

chapters.title: Standing at the crossroads
chapters.text:
The trap of living a double life and the discomfort of split loyalty inside the Ukrainian elite was only half the story. Even among writers who stayed committed to Ukraine, there were loud arguments about which way to go next. Once you turned your back on the imperial model, what did you turn toward? Young Ukrainian writers kept hashing that question out among themselves.

Ivan Nechui-Levytsky saw the village as the seedbed for keeping ancient ways alive and for nurturing a thriving modern Ukrainian culture. For him, the countryside, the customs, and the cultural achievements raised there were the only real hope for keeping the Ukrainian identity intact.

By contrast, the city looked to him like a foreign space, a place the empire had captured. Anything urban struck him as a tool of occupation, a way of seeding Russian ideology in everyday life.

The scholar Mykhailo Drahomanov read the situation differently. He felt the knee-jerk rejection of Russian literature, fed by the Ukrainian "culture under threat" complex, was a dead end. He believed Ukrainian literature had to be built on a stronger foundation, and that to do so, writers needed to reach past Russia and take ideas straight from the West.

By the close of the Romantic period, the rising generation of Ukrainian modernists was already tilting toward the West, and the dread of foreign influence had faded. Even with the Valuev Circular of 1863 and the Ems Ukaz of 1876 clamping down, the bold Ukrainian intelligentsia found ways to channel its creative fire. With help from the Old Community, a kind of shadow government for Ukraine, Ukrainian-language works were printed abroad in Galicia and even slipped into the empire itself, where they quickly found an audience along the Dnipro.

Members of the Community slipped money to a corrupt St. Petersburg censor, who was happy to look the other way on pro-Ukrainian publications.

The Community wove together a network of scientific, cultural, and educational institutions aimed at growing a new, devoted national elite and pulling the Ukrainian community into a single fabric. The fiery Lesya Ukrainka had no patience for fairy tales about brotherly nations. She stood out as a sharp representative of the contemporary Ukrainian intelligentsia, the intellectuals, and a determined opponent of the Russian Empire. As she saw it, the only thing the peoples of the imperial territories shared was a common yoke.

Lesya Ukrainka was among the first to be stirred by Western ideas and plots, and she openly criticized the cult of Shevchenko for its bunker mentality. She reached instead for widely accepted European values and pinned Ukraine's future on them.

In her view, a nation's fate rested on the intellectual elite and on what that elite carried to the people. That is why Lesya Ukrainka turned against the fashions of her time and refused to dwell on peasant, traditional, and Ukrainophile themes. Her stories unfurled across continents, never trapping the reader in chains of serfdom, and instead offering historical examples of people breaking loose.

chapters.title: The red fever, heir to the dark empire
chapters.text:
The uprising of the Ukrainian People's Republic in the early twentieth century lifted the national spirit and united the people in a new way. The strength of the Ukrainian people scared the new Russian government, which could not allow a country with such rich lands to slip away. After the republic was defeated and the Bolsheviks took power, ideologues began demonizing Ukrainians.

Mikhail Bulgakov, a native of Kyiv, joined the propaganda effort. His novel The White Guard describes the capture of his hometown by the Red Army under Mikhail Muravyov in February 1918. The winter of 1918 is one of the darkest pages in Ukrainian history. The Battle of Kruty took place in those months.

When a Bolshevik force of roughly 4,000 men under Muravyov marched toward Kyiv, a small contingent of 500 men was sent to the front. It was made up of a company from the Student Battalion of Sich Riflemen, a company from the Khmelnytsky Cadet School, and a Haidamaka detachment. The battle is remembered as a symbol of patriotic self-sacrifice, immortalized in countless literary and journalistic works.

Despite the Ukrainian sacrifice, the Russians wiped out the leadership of the Ukrainian People's Republic, and Muravyov's Red Terror swept into Kyiv. Russian officers who staged a pogrom, or massacre, in the city used chemical weapons and, depending on the estimate, killed two to five thousand residents. They were celebrated as the saviors of the capital and defenders of "unity."

The Ukrainian creative elite once again found itself at a crossroads. Some, including Mykola Zerov and Maksym Rylsky, treated the proletarian government as the rightful heir to the empire. They followed the path laid out by their predecessors in the Old Community, working to develop and enrich Ukraine, drawing on European ideas. Others, including Mykola Khvylovy and Mykola Kulish, were drawn to socialist and communist ideas and joined the ruling party. At first, they trusted the Bolshevik promises. After seeing the flood of red propaganda written under party orders and the humiliation of Ukrainian culture, they realized they had to run, fast, away from Moscow.

The Ukrainian intelligentsia pulled together again in the western direction and kept battling Russian toadies and invaders. Cultural scandals around rebellious Ukraine kept popping up. The most famous is the clash between Volodymyr Vinnychenko and Oleksa Slisarenko against Maxim Gorky. Gorky refused to let his novel Mother be translated into Ukrainian, dismissing the language as "an idiom." He got a sharp literary rebuke from his Ukrainian colleagues and a chilly reception during his visit to Kharkiv.

Ukrainization was in its active phase, and the red apparatus and the censors could not keep up. The Soviet authorities realized that Ukrainian authors would never become propaganda writers like Bulgakov, and that their national spirit could not be broken or bought. They took relations with them to a new level, one that involved executions, prison cells, and exile.

From May 1933 onward, uninvited guests showed up at the Slovo building in Kharkiv every night. The Slovo building was an apartment block for Ukraine's creative elite and intelligentsia. After each visit, the number of Slovo residents went down. Later historians would call this terrible page of Ukrainian history the Executed Renaissance.

If you want to learn more about the life of writers in the capital Kharkiv in the 1930s, the summary of A Word About the Writers' Home "Slovo" by Volodymyr Kulish is recommended in the Headway app.

The Soviet authorities were so afraid of Mykola Khvylovy's influence that they dug up his grave in the Kharkiv city cemetery and built an amusement park on top of it.

Khvylovy had been a member of the literary organization VAPLITE, which he founded in 1925. The group wanted to pull Ukrainian letters away from Russian imitation and toward the rest of Europe. The Soviets considered VAPLITE a hotbed of nationalist drift, and the publishing house behind it was shut down by 1928. Khvylovy kept writing underground for a few more years before taking his own life in 1933, just before the Slovo raids reached their peak.

Les Kurbas, the leading Ukrainian theater director of the era, was arrested in 1933 and shot in 1937. His name was left off official Soviet lists of important directors for decades, and many of his plays were only re-staged in independent Ukraine after 1991.

chapters.title: How to break free from cultural and historical amnesia
chapters.text:
It is always the winners who write history, and the Soviet government was no exception. Ideologues and political technologists burned the midnight oil rewriting the history of Ukraine and Russia, weaving the two stories together and inventing links that never existed, all in the name of binding two very different peoples into one tidy narrative.

Ukrainians, though, kept digging for the truth and for proof of what made them different from their occupiers. Viktor Domontovych's Soilless ranks among the most important works of literature to argue for the preservation and institutionalization of national memory. The author insists that memory, archaeology, and the physical cultural monuments left standing are the proof and the foundation of Ukrainian distinctiveness.

One of the clearest examples of the Soviet campaign against Ukrainian culture is the construction of the Dnipro Hydroelectric Station. The red regime deliberately flooded the legendary Dnipro rapids, along with ancient settlements, burial sites, and Cossack churches.

The whole Russification project started to lose steam when the Second World War began. Slogans about the "great Soviet motherland" could not rally scattered and distant peoples. To counter the Nazi threat, the Soviet authorities relaxed censorship and gave creators partial freedom, hoping the loosening would help unite and motivate people to fight the Third Reich.

The war briefly opened the door to a small Ukrainian cultural renaissance in the early 1940s. The lightning-fast occupation of the Ukrainian SSR and the heavy losses, though, only added fuel to Muscovite hatred of Ukraine. Alongside the partial easing of censorship, the Moscow propaganda machine kept running at full speed, hammering home the image of the "Ukrainian traitor" and the "collaborationist."

The work of Oleksandr Dovzhenko, a celebrated Ukrainian screenwriter, film producer, and director, captures another kind of threat to Ukrainian identity. In his diary, the author wrote, "We were the only nation in Europe that did not know who we were."

After the great victory, things snapped back to where they had been. Censorship ran at full capacity, and the freer works of the war years were quickly rewritten and republished.

A fresh wave of repression swept through Ukraine, and after it, every Ukrainian author's "good friend" arrived, the identity crisis. Defining yourself as a Ukrainian and keeping your distance from the Soviet collective brought death and destruction. Defining yourself as a Russian or Soviet person brought pain and a break from who you really were. Most authors had to wear masks, going through the motions of party orders while quietly producing something genuine, something Ukrainian, and something of real value.

For decades, the Ukrainian intelligentsia leaned on the philosophy of valenrodism, a kind of pseudo-assimilation into the enemy's ranks aimed at tearing them down from the inside. That approach probably played a decisive role in keeping the Ukrainian identity alive.

A small story worth knowing. The writer Mykhailo Drai-Khmara told a famous tale from a prison cell about Mykola Khvylovy, who buried a suitcase full of his manuscripts somewhere in a village in the Slobozhan region before taking his own life.

chapters.title: Rebirth
chapters.text:
The dream of freedom finally arrived in 1991, and with it, the end of the masquerade and the forced worship of the Soviet machine. The pens of Ukrainian authors were finally free of the red shackles.

At the turn of the millennium, Ukrainian writers felt they had to speak on behalf of at least three generations, the generations that had been silenced. That line comes from Vira Ageyeva.

The work of Valery Shevchuk and Oksana Zabuzhko became iconic in this effort. Shevchuk is a literary and cultural archaeologist. His main interest is in the options and possibilities of conserving and preserving memories, stories, and plots. He sees them as the source of national memory.

In his novel Three Leaves Behind the Window, Shevchuk walks readers through the story of three generations of one family as colonized culture falls apart. We see the hero's fear of uncertainty and his inability to figure out who he is, as he tries to fit into imperial life by renouncing the heritage of his own community. Even mentioning prominent ancestors threatens his ability to identify with the occupiers. The horror of those realities stirs up the urge to protect his own culture.

Ukrainian historical novels of the twentieth century tended to focus on sweeping, grand narratives, putting big events and major figures on the page. Young writers of the new millennium prefer micro-stories.

Soviet historians in 1943 were so lost in their own invented world that they called the first official king of Ukraine, Daniel of Galicia, a Russian Kniaz and patriot who had defended the "Russian language" for centuries.

It was impossible to trust the official narrative put out over the previous three centuries, so writers turned to private, small, personal stories that almost every family knew. That approach lets readers see how global events landed on ordinary people and how those people made sense of what was happening to them.

A great example of that kind of work is Oksana Zabuzhko's Museum of Abandoned Secrets, a detective story about characters who investigate the history of their own families and, in the process, the history of Ukraine. Global political events and natural disasters shaped the lives of their ancestors.

Oksana Zabuzhko believes writing is actually one of the least reliable ways of preserving information, since manuscripts tend to be burned and destroyed. What is buried in the ground, on the other hand, can help rebuild a picture of the past.

A short tour of the field is useful. Yuriy Vynnychuk has built a reputation on dense, often dark historical fiction that mixes magic and political satire. Yuri Andrukhovych, born in Ivano-Frankivsk, writes in a postmodern key and is closely watched in the German-speaking literary world, where most of his books have been translated. Andrey Kurkov, whose novels often walk the line between absurdism and realism, has become one of the most translated Ukrainian authors of his generation, with his Death and the Penguin appearing in more than thirty languages.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The cultural relationship between Ukraine and Russia is a long history of one developed European culture being enslaved and conquered by descendants of the Tatar-Mongol yoke. The sharp difference between Ukrainians and Russians was already clear in the seventeenth century. Centuries of occupation, expansion, and violent appropriation by the empire and the Soviet Union did nothing to close the gap in evolution, culture, and education.

Eyewitness stories about the lives of Soviet occupiers in European Lviv prove the point. The wives of senior Soviet officers, once they moved into Galician homes, ate lipsticks because they thought the sticks were candy and went to the theater in nightgowns because they thought the gowns were evening dresses. The high-ranking officers themselves wore huge wall clocks on their wrists, stole toilets, and rolled through the city center day after day with wheelbarrows full of looted "bourgeois property." It sounds hard to believe that an officer would behave that way, but for the Russians, it was ordinary.

In the twenty-first century, Ukraine finally got the chance to declare itself, to speak out, and to talk about everything the occupiers had done on its land for centuries. The rehabilitation of historical and cultural figures has been a breath of fresh air, a kind of resurrection of what Russia so carefully and methodically destroyed.

For the first time in the last three hundred years, the world is being introduced to the true history of Ukraine. The young creative elite has torn down Russian narratives about Ukrainian culture, and the country is growing and showing itself on the world stage.

A small thing to try.

Find early editions of famous Ukrainian authors whose work was savaged by Soviet censorship and read their real thoughts, for example, the first edition of Taras Bulba by Mykola Hohol. If the history of Ukrainian culture interests you, keep going with Headway's Essence of Ukraine project.
